raw_case:
Right-sided Poland syndrome with no classical hand deformity: A case report
Poland syndrome (PS) is a rare congenital condition characterized by the developmental anomaly of the chest wall, and classically presents with ipsilateral agenesis/hypoplasia of sternocostal head of pectoralis major which remains as the essential feature of the condition. It may or may not be associated with ipsilateral limb abnormalities. Diagnosis is mainly clinical. Imaging modalities like chest X-Ray, CT chest, US, and 3D-VRT CT can be used to know about the extent of involvement of this anomaly. We hereby present a case of a 25-year-old male who visited our hospital with chief complaint of poor development of right-sided chest wall, who was later diagnosed with right-sided Poland syndrome with no classical hand deformity. Every physician must put a differential diagnosis of PS in the back of mind while dealing with the case of poor development of chest wall.

Introduction

Poland Syndrome (PS) is a rare congenital condition characterized by the developmental anomaly of the chest wall, and classically presents with ipsilateral agenesis/hypoplasia of sternocostal head of pectoralis major, multiple rib abnormalities, elevated and rotated scapula (Sprengel deformity), hypoplasia of nipple or breast, absence of subcutaneous fat and ipsilateral digital abnormalities (brachydactyly, syndactyly) [1]. However, the combination of these features is rarely found in a single individual [2]. In most of the cases, PS presents with unilateral hypoplasia of the breast and pectoralis major muscle, which remains as the essential criteria of the condition [3]. PS has an estimated incidence of 1 birth per 30,000 births [4]. PS occurs predominantly in males with one third of the cases affecting the right side [5]. The exact etiology of PS is still unknown. However, it is believed to occur due to vascularization arrest during the fetal development [5].We hereby present a case of a 25-year-old male who visited our hospital with chief complaint of poor development of right-sided chest wall, who was later diagnosed with right-sided Poland syndrome with no classical hand deformity.

Case presentation

A 25-year -old male was brought to our hospital with chief complaint of poor development of right-sided chest wall. He was born out of nonconsanguineous marriage by a mother of 28 years of age via normal vaginal delivery. His developmental milestones were all achieved according to his peers. He had good school performance. There were no any facial abnormalities. There was no any history of smoking, alcohol consumption, and drug abuse in the mother during pregnancy. The pregnancy was uneventful with 2 antenatal US being done which did not report any anomalies. There was no family history of similar presentation in any of the family members.On physical examination the vitals were within normal limits. There was poor development of right-sided chest wall with nonpalpation of pectoralis major muscle during adduction of arm against resistance. The serratus anterior muscle of right side was also not palpable. Both the clavicular and sternocostal head of pectoralis muscle and the serratus anterior muscle of the left side was palpable. The nipple of right side was not visualized. There were no any hand and limb abnormalities like shortened finger digits, webbed fingers, and hypoplasia of thenar and hypothenar muscles of the ipsilateral side. Rest of the systemic examination were nonsignificant. Based on these clinical findings, suspicion of Poland syndrome was made and CXR and CT chest was advised for further evaluation.Chest X-ray revealed minimally hyperlucent right lung field with asymmetry in right upper ribs. There was no evidence of dextrocardia (Fig. 1). CT scan of the chest showed absence of right pectoralis major and minor muscle (Fig. 2), absence of right serratus anterior muscle (Fig. 4), and absence of right breast tissue (Fig. 3). Bilateral lung parenchyma was normal on CT scan (Fig. 5) proving the fact that, the hyperlucent lung on CXR was due to chest wall deformity rather than parenchymal abnormalities. Anterior aspect of right second and third rib was hypoplastic which was better visualized in 3D reconstructed bone VRT image of the chest wall (Fig. 6). Based on the constellation of above findings, the diagnosis of right sided Poland syndrome with no classical hand deformity was made. All the treatment options were explained to the patient's guardians but they decided to go with observation and follow up and not to undergo any surgical intervention. On follow up, the patient was asymptomatic with no any respiratory symptoms.

Discussion

The first description about PS was provided by French Lallem and in 1826. But the credit for the first complete description about this malformation goes to the British Surgeon, Doctor Alfred Poland [5]. PS affects men mostly with the ratio of 2:1 to 3:1 ratio. The right side of the body is affected in 60% to 75% of patients, with bilateral involvement occurring in less than 1% of cases [6].Studies have proposed 3 theories for the etiology of PS: genetic, teratogenic, or fetal vascular compromise [7]. PS is usually sporadic and the familial connection is less than 1% [8]. Studies show that the children have twofold risks of developing PS whose mothers smoked during pregnancy [9]. However, the most accepted theory for the pathogenesis of PS is the fetal vascular compromise theory which suggests that, PS may be due to disruption of the development of the proximal subclavian artery that supplies the pectoral muscles around the sixth week of gestation. This causes decreased blood flow to the pectoral region and distal limb, leading to regional loss of tissue [10]. The term ‘subclavian artery supply disruption sequence (SASDS)’ is used to describe the disruption of the blood vessels, suggesting a common pathogenesis in Poland syndrome and Moebius syndromes [11]. Several factors like environmental and mechanical may also cause interruption or reduction in blood flow to the subclavian artery and its branches. Intrinsic factors may include thrombi and emboli, while the extrinsic factors may include cervical rib, aberrant muscle, amniotic bands can cause vascular disruption [12].There are many modes of presentation of PS: Type 1 (minimal form) refers an isolated pectoral muscle defect, Type 2 (partial form) refers a pectoral muscle defect associated with either an upper limb (2a, upper limb variant), or a rib (2b, thoracic variant) anomaly, and Type 3 (complete form) refers a pectoral muscle defect with both upper limb and rib anomalies [7]. Ours was a case of Type 2b variant of PS with pectoral muscle defect and rib anomaly. The thoracic abnormalities involved in Poland syndrome are of 3 types: muscular, osteo-cartilaginous and cutaneous glandular. On the muscular level, agenesis of the sternocostal heads of the pectoralis major muscle constitutes the most constant anomaly of the syndrome. This agenesis may be associated with hypoplasia of the other muscles of the thorax on the ipsilateral side. Damage to the sternocostal portions of the second, third, fourth, and fifth ribs constitute the main osteo-cartilaginous lesions. It is a hypoplasia with extreme aplasia. Cutaneo-glandular abnormalities are made of thin with solid adhesions between the dermis and the chest wall [13]. There may be other associated features, such as underdevelopment or absence of 1 nipple including the areola and/or patchy absence of hair in the axilla [14]. In females, there may be underdevelopment or aplasia of 1 breast and underlying (subcutaneous) tissues [15]. Other associated skeletal abnormalities may include underdevelopment or absence of upper ribs; elevation of the shoulder blade (Sprengel deformity); and/or shortening of the arm, with underdevelopment of the forearm bones (i.e., ulna and radius) [14]. The case we presented had features pertaining to muscular defect (absence of pectoralis major, minor and serratus anterior muscle), osteo-cartilaginous defect (hypoplastic anterior second and third ribs), and cutaneo-glandular defect (hypoplasia of right sided nipple). After a study of 10 patients, a clinical and radiological classification of thoracic musculoskeletal anomalies associated with PS was proposed in 2011 [13]. This classification consists of 4 categories: • first degree: Hypoplasia of the pectoralis muscles. • second degree: Absence of the sternocostal head of the major pectoralis muscle. • third degree: Total absence of the major pectoralis muscle or both pectoralis muscles. • fourth degree: Hypoplasia or absence of the pectoralis muscles combined with skeletal anomalies of the thoracic bones (sternum or rib cage) [13]. Our case had fourth degree musculoskeletal anomaly (hypoplasia of pectoralis muscles combined with hypoplasia of right anterior second and third ribs).The diagnosis of PS is entirely clinical. Imaging modalities are required to confirm the clinical diagnosis, to know about associated malformations, and to decide the therapeutic intervention to be performed in the patient [16]. Chest radiograph may reveal hyperlucent lung fields on the affected side with heart displaced towards the unaffected side [17]. According to the literature, it looks like “a post radical mastectomy picture” [18]. To confirm the clinical diagnosis of PS, Ultrasound (US) represents the first line diagnostic imaging method, because of its easy accessibility, cost-effective and radiation free modality. It provides an accurate description of soft tissue and chest wall morphological abnormalities, which is difficult to identify during clinical assessment [18]. US evaluation is needed to assess pectoralis muscle (major and minor), breast characteristics of the 2 halves of the thorax, and could be used as screening exam for cardiac and renal abnormalities [18]. Most cases of moderate to severe PS are diagnosed at birth when presented with visible chest or limb defects. Antenatal diagnosis using fetal ultrasound scan (US) can detect limb or thoracic defects. Doppler assessment of ipsilateral subclavian artery flow is recommended when unilateral skeletal defects are detected in fetal US. Three-dimensional US can aid in characterization of the limb defect [19]. Mammograms reveal unilateral breast and pectoralis major hypoplasia [20]. Modalities like CT and MRI provide accurate details about the absence of the pectoralis major muscle and allows better appreciations of other nearby associated musculoskeletal and inner organs anomalies. But due to the radiation exposure in CT, it is not usually preferred for presurgical planning. In setting of presurgical planning, MRI can provide the most of necessary information without exposing patient, often in young age, to the risk of radiations [21]. Advanced modalities like 3D-CT demonstrates the extent of the deformity and tissue hypoplasia in the most accurate way, which helps with planning for transposition of muscular flaps and reconstructive techniques [18]. The imaging modalities used in our case was CXR, CT chest and 3D-VRT CT which showed the accurate details regarding the extent of anomalies.Indications of surgery in case of PS include: (a) Unilateral depression of the chest wall with possible progression with age, (b) Inadequate protection of the lung and heart, (c) Paradoxical movement of the chest wall, and (d) for cosmetic reasons owing to the absence of breast and pectoralis muscle and anterior axillary fold [22]. Treatment is based on the severity of the deformity. If there is no severe manifestation or functional deformity, no surgical intervention is needed. Surgical treatment options include reconstruction of the chest wall with myo-cutaneous flap coverage and breast augmentation procedure [23]. Other procedures like Ipsilateral pedicled latissimus dorsi flap and submuscular augmentation can also be done. For syndactyly, syndactyly reconstruction of affected digits is done, especially if the thumb is involved in improving grasping and pinching function [24]. There were no any indications for surgery in our patient, so he was advised for wait and watch strategy with routine follow up.

Conclusion

PS is a rare congenital disorder with varied clinical spectrum. Its classical presentation is poor development of chest wall with or without limb abnormalities. Presence of limb abnormalities may not be present in most of the cases. The diagnosis is purely clinical. Use of imaging modalities is required to know the extent of involvement of chest wall defects. Every physician must put a differential diagnosis of PS in the back of mind while dealing with the case of poor development of chest wall.

Ethical approval

The study is exempt from ethical approval in our institution.

Provenance and peer review

Not commissioned, externally peer-reviewed.

Patient consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for publication of this case report and accompanying images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal on request.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 25-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Poor development of the right-sided chest wall
- **History of Present Illness:** None
- **Past Medical History:** None
- **Personal History:** None; no smoking, alcohol consumption, or drug use by the mother during pregnancy
- **Family History:** None; no similar presentations in family members
- **Physical Examination:**
  - Nonpalpable right-sided pectoralis major, serratus anterior, and sternocostal head of pectoralis muscle during resistance testing
  - Nonvisualization of the right nipple
  - No abnormalities of the hands or limbs
  - Vitals and systemic examination normal
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Chest X-ray: Hyperlucent right lung field, asymmetry in upper ribs, no dextrocardia
  - CT Chest: Absence of right pectoralis major, minor, and serratus anterior muscles; hypoplastic anterior second and third ribs; absence of right breast tissue; bilateral lung parenchyma normal
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Step 1:** Consider poor chest wall development:
  - Poland Syndrome (PS) is a potential diagnosis due to hallmark features like pectoralis major agenesis and associated chest wall anomalies.
  - Exclusion of other congenital syndromes (e.g., Sprengel deformity was absent; there were no systemic symptoms or limb abnormalities).
- **Step 2:** Evaluate imaging findings:
  - Absence of specific chest muscles (e.g., pectoralis major and serratus anterior) and hypoplastic ribs strongly supports PS.
  - Normal lung parenchyma and absence of heart displacement ruled out parenchymal or cardiovascular pathologies.
- **Step 3:** Rule out limb defects and alternative causes:
  - Lack of hand deformities or digital abnormalities (e.g., syndactyly, brachydactyly) excludes certain subtypes of PS and other syndromic conditions.
- **Step 4:** Confirm final pathology:
  - Imaging confirmed muscular, osteo-cartilaginous, and cutaneous glandular defects consistent with a diagnosis of Poland Syndrome without classical limb anomalies (Type 2b variant).
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Right-sided Poland Syndrome (PS) with no accompanying classical hand or upper limb abnormalities, Type 2b variant. The diagnosis is clinically supported by imaging findings of chest wall deformities including muscle absence and rib hypoplasia.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> Initial assessment of the chief complaint and patient demographics: A 25-year-old male presenting with poor development of the right-sided chest wall. This points towards a congenital or developmental issue rather than an acquired condition, given the long-standing nature of "poor development." The patient is otherwise healthy with no significant medical, personal, or family history.

<step 2> Analysis of physical examination findings: The physical exam reveals the absence of palpable right-sided pectoralis major, serratus anterior, and sternocostal head of pectoralis muscle during resistance testing, and non-visualization of the right nipple.  This strongly suggests a deficiency in the development of these structures on the right side.  The absence of hand or limb abnormalities is noted, which is important for differential diagnosis.  Normal vitals and systemic examination suggest the issue is localized to the chest wall.

<step 3> Interpretation of ancillary tests: The chest X-ray shows a hyperlucent right lung field, which is consistent with a lack of soft tissue in the anterior chest wall allowing for increased X-ray penetration.  Asymmetry in upper ribs is also noted, further supporting a structural abnormality.  The absence of dextrocardia rules out a more complex midline developmental issue. The CT chest is crucial: it confirms the absence of right pectoralis major, minor, and serratus anterior muscles, along with hypoplastic anterior second and third ribs and the absence of right breast tissue.  This detailed imaging corroborates the physical exam and provides precise anatomical information.  Normal lung parenchyma rules out primary lung pathology.

<step 4> Synthesis of findings and differential diagnosis: The combination of absent pectoralis major, minor, and serratus anterior muscles, hypoplastic ribs, and absent breast tissue, all on the right side, strongly suggests a specific congenital condition. Considering the unilateral nature and the specific muscles involved, Poland Syndrome becomes the most prominent diagnostic consideration.  While isolated pectoralis major agenesis could be considered, the involvement of serratus anterior, pectoralis minor, ribs, and breast tissue makes Poland Syndrome more likely. Other conditions like muscular dystrophy or neuromuscular disorders are less likely given the congenital and localized nature of the findings and the absence of progressive weakness or neurological symptoms. Trauma is ruled out by the history.

<step 5> Final Diagnostic Conclusion: Based on the constellation of clinical findings, physical examination, and definitive CT scan results, the most fitting diagnosis is Poland Syndrome. This syndrome is characterized by the absence or hypoplasia of the pectoralis major muscle, and can be associated with other chest wall anomalies including absence of pectoralis minor and serratus anterior, rib abnormalities, and breast/nipple hypoplasia or aplasia, all of which are present in this case.  While Poland Syndrome can sometimes involve hand anomalies, their absence in this case does not exclude the diagnosis.

### Answer:
Poland Syndrome

reasoning_eval.step: Initial assessment of the chief complaint and patient demographics: A 25-year-old male presenting with poor development of the right-sided chest wall. This points towards a congenital or developmental issue rather than an acquired condition, given the long-standing nature of "poor development." The patient is otherwise healthy with no significant medical, personal, or family history.

Analysis of physical examination findings: The physical exam reveals the absence of palpable right-sided pectoralis major, serratus anterior, and sternocostal head of pectoralis muscle during resistance testing, and non-visualization of the right nipple.  This strongly suggests a deficiency in the development of these structures on the right side.  The absence of hand or limb abnormalities is noted, which is important for differential diagnosis.  Normal vitals and systemic examination suggest the issue is localized to the chest wall.

Interpretation of ancillary tests: The chest X-ray shows a hyperlucent right lung field, which is consistent with a lack of soft tissue in the anterior chest wall allowing for increased X-ray penetration.  Asymmetry in upper ribs is also noted, further supporting a structural abnormality.  The absence of dextrocardia rules out a more complex midline developmental issue. The CT chest is crucial: it confirms the absence of right pectoralis major, minor, and serratus anterior muscles, along with hypoplastic anterior second and third ribs and the absence of right breast tissue.  This detailed imaging corroborates the physical exam and provides precise anatomical information.  Normal lung parenchyma rules out primary lung pathology.

Synthesis of findings and differential diagnosis: The combination of absent pectoralis major, minor, and serratus anterior muscles, hypoplastic ribs, and absent breast tissue, all on the right side, strongly suggests a specific congenital condition. Considering the unilateral nature and the specific muscles involved, Poland Syndrome becomes the most prominent diagnostic consideration.  While isolated pectoralis major agenesis could be considered, the involvement of serratus anterior, pectoralis minor, ribs, and breast tissue makes Poland Syndrome more likely. Other conditions like muscular dystrophy or neuromuscular disorders are less likely given the congenital and localized nature of the findings and the absence of progressive weakness or neurological symptoms. Trauma is ruled out by the history.

Final Diagnostic Conclusion: Based on the constellation of clinical findings, physical examination, and definitive CT scan results, the most fitting diagnosis is Poland Syndrome. This syndrome is characterized by the absence or hypoplasia of the pectoralis major muscle, and can be associated with other chest wall anomalies including absence of pectoralis minor and serratus anterior, rib abnormalities, and breast/nipple hypoplasia or aplasia, all of which are present in this case.  While Poland Syndrome can sometimes involve hand anomalies, their absence in this case does not exclude the diagnosis.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Consider poor chest wall development: Poland Syndrome (PS) is a potential diagnosis due to hallmark features like pectoralis major agenesis and associated chest wall anomalies, excluding other congenital syndromes such as Sprengel deformity and systemic symptoms or limb abnormalities.

Evaluate imaging findings: Absence of specific chest muscles (e.g., pectoralis major and serratus anterior) and hypoplastic ribs strongly supports PS, while normal lung parenchyma and absence of heart displacement rule out parenchymal or cardiovascular pathologies.
Rule out limb defects and alternative causes: Lack of hand deformities or digital abnormalities (e.g., syndactyly, brachydactyly) excludes certain subtypes of PS and other syndromic conditions.

Confirm final pathology: Imaging confirmed muscular, osteo-cartilaginous, and cutaneous glandular defects consistent with a diagnosis of Poland Syndrome without classical limb anomalies (Type 2b variant).

Final diagnosis: Right-sided Poland Syndrome (PS) with no accompanying classical hand or upper limb abnormalities, Type 2b variant. The diagnosis is clinically supported by imaging findings of chest wall deformities including muscle absence and rib hypoplasia.
